weather.current_meta: Feels like 35°C · Low 21°C · Rain <5% · Gusts 26 mph · Humidity 40%

warnings.title: Met Office Red Warning — Extreme Heat
warnings.body_html: An exceptional spell of hot weather covers central and southern England, including Surrey, until 21:00 Thursday 25 June, with temperatures forecast up to 39°C in the warmest spots. The Met Office says impacts to the general population are likely.

warnings.title: UKHSA Red Heat-Health Alert — South East England
warnings.body_html: A red heat-health alert is active for the South East, including Surrey, through to 23:00 Friday 26 June. The UKHSA says a heatwave of this severity carries a risk to health across the whole population and may disrupt services.

days.sum: Peak of the heatwave — strong sunshine, up to 35°C and a warm night.
Still very hot early as Atlantic air pushes in — turning fresher with a risk of thundery showers.
Noticeably cooler and fresher with sunny spells and the odd passing shower.
Back to comfortable warmth with sunshine, cloud and scattered showers.
Pleasant and largely dry with light winds and good sunny spells.
More cloud around but staying mild and mostly dry.
Sunshine returns with comfortable summer warmth and light winds.

sources.title: Red Extreme Heat Warning issued as June records forecast to break
UKHSA extends red heat-health alerts across England

hero.headline: Twin earthquakes near Caracas kill at least 164, Venezuela's acting president says
hero.summary: An M7.2 foreshock and an M7.5 mainshock 39 seconds apart struck northern Venezuela on 24 June, killing at least 164 and injuring 971 by the government's early-Thursday count, with the toll expected to rise.

cards.headline: US and Iran set 60-day roadmap; dispute inspections as Israel–Lebanon talks run in Washington
cards.summary: US and Iranian negotiators concluded Switzerland talks on a 60-day roadmap toward a final deal, with four working groups and a Strait of Hormuz communication line, while the two sides publicly disagreed over the timing of nuclear inspections.

cards.headline: France records hottest day on national scale since 1947 as Europe heat peaks
cards.summary: France's national temperature indicator reached 30.0°C on 24 June — its highest since the measure began in 1947 — and Météo-France placed 72 departments under red heatwave alert for 25 June as extreme heat extended across western Europe.

cards.headline: AI memory chips lift Asia after Micron's record quarter; oil holds below $70
cards.summary: Asian markets closed higher on 25 June on a memory-chip rally after Micron's record fiscal Q3, while oil stayed near four-month lows and the Fed's mid-June hold framed the rate backdrop.

cards.headline: Colombia's electoral council certifies de la Espriella as president-elect after challenge rejected
cards.summary: Colombia's electoral council on 24 June officially declared conservative Abelardo de la Espriella the winner of the 21 June runoff over Iván Cepeda, who conceded after the council rejected his party's challenge to roughly 33,000 polling stations.

cards.headline: Asian memory-chip shares rise after Micron's record quarter; SK Hynix plans Nasdaq listing
cards.summary: AI-memory chip stocks across Asia rose in the 25 June session after Micron reported record fiscal third-quarter revenue of $41.46bn and SK Hynix disclosed a plan to raise up to about $29.4bn in a US listing.

cards.headline: CDC ends response to cruise-ship hantavirus outbreak after US monitoring clears
cards.summary: The US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention said on 24 June it had concluded its response to an Andes virus outbreak tied to the cruise ship M/V Hondius, after all US-exposed travellers completed a 42-day monitoring period without illness.

cards.headline: Scotland lose 0-3 to Brazil and finish third in Group C; qualification out of their hands
cards.summary: Brazil beat Scotland 3-0 through two Vinícius Júnior goals and one from Matheus Cunha, leaving Scotland third in Group C on three points and dependent on other groups to advance as a best third-placed side.

stories.headline: Twin earthquakes near Caracas kill at least 164, Venezuela's acting president says
stories.summary: An M7.2 foreshock and an M7.5 mainshock 39 seconds apart struck northern Venezuela on 24 June, killing at least 164 and injuring 971 by the government's early-Thursday count, with the toll expected to rise.

confidence.label: Developing
confidence.why: — core facts (magnitudes, sequence, location, US and Chinese responses) are multi-sourced, but the casualty tally is rising and single-sourced to the government

image.alt: Panoramic view of the Caracas city skyline with high-rise buildings and the Ávila mountain behind.
image.caption: The skyline of Caracas, where buildings collapsed in the 24 June 2026 earthquakes. Photo: City Vzla · CC BY-SA 4.0

inline_image.alt: Physical relief map of Venezuela showing topography, the Caribbean coast and the capital region.
inline_image.caption: A relief map of Venezuela; the 24 June epicentres lay in the north, near Yumare in Yaracuy state, roughly 160 km west of Caracas. Photo: Sémhur / NordNordWest / Milenioscuro · CC BY-SA 3.0

article_blocks.html: Two earthquakes struck northern Venezuela on the evening of Wednesday 24 June 2026, killing at least 164 people and injuring 971, acting President Delcy Rodríguez said early Thursday. The figures were reported by CNN, Al Jazeera, NPR and NBC News and remained the official government count as of 25 June.

The United States Geological Survey (USGS) recorded a magnitude 7.2 foreshock followed 39 seconds later by a magnitude 7.5 mainshock. The USGS placed the larger event near Yumare in Yaracuy state, roughly 100 miles (about 160 km) west of Caracas, at shallow depth; an initial reading of M7.1 was revised to M7.2 and the second event put at M7.5.

Rodríguez said her office received reports of building collapses across Caracas and several states, including Carabobo, Aragua, Miranda, Trujillo and La Guaira. She described the coastal state of La Guaira, north of the capital, as a "disaster zone", and said its reported casualties were not yet included in the national count.

Infrastructure was disrupted across the affected region. Reports cited by Al Jazeera said Simón Bolívar International Airport, which serves Caracas, was damaged and flights were cancelled; the Caracas Metro and rail services were suspended; and power and telecommunications failed in parts of the country. Rodríguez said school classes were suspended nationwide.

article_blocks.head: Timeline — how it unfolded
article_blocks.sub: Tap an event to expand. Spacing reflects the time between events.

events.label: 24 Jun
events.title: M7.2 foreshock
events.detail: A magnitude 7.2 earthquake struck near Yaracuy state at about 6:04 p.m. local time (22:04 GMT).

events.label: 24 Jun
events.title: M7.5 mainshock
events.detail: A magnitude 7.5 earthquake followed 39 seconds later in the same area, near Yumare west of Caracas.

events.label: 24 Jun
events.title: Tsunami advisory
events.detail: The US tsunami warning system issued, then cancelled, a tsunami advisory for Puerto Rico and the US Virgin Islands.

events.label: 24 Jun
events.title: US response
events.detail: Secretary of State Marco Rubio said the US was deploying search-and-rescue teams from Fairfax County, Virginia, and Los Angeles.

events.label: 25 Jun
events.title: Toll reported
events.detail: Acting President Delcy Rodríguez put the count at at least 164 dead and 971 injured, excluding La Guaira, and said it was expected to rise.

article_blocks.html: The US National Weather Service's tsunami warning system issued a tsunami advisory for Puerto Rico and the US Virgin Islands after the quakes, according to CNN and CBS News; the advisories were later cancelled.

article_blocks.text: "We are already deploying search and rescue teams from Fairfax County, Virginia, and Los Angeles."
article_blocks.src: — Marco Rubio, US Secretary of State, 24 June 2026

article_blocks.html: US Secretary of State Marco Rubio said on 24 June that the United States was deploying search-and-rescue teams and medical resources. China, a major buyer of Venezuelan oil, said it would assist; Rodríguez said offers of aid had also come from Brazil, El Salvador, Ecuador, Panama, France, Switzerland and Caribbean nations.

The USGS PAGER system issued a red alert for both events, its highest level, indicating that high casualties and extensive damage are probable. Its modelling estimated a 39% probability of 1,000–10,000 deaths and a 37% probability of 10,000–100,000 deaths, and projected economic losses most likely between $10 billion and $100 billion. The 164 confirmed deaths reflect only counts relayed by authorities so far.

Why it matters: The M7.5 mainshock is, according to USGS data cited by multiple outlets, the strongest earthquake to strike Venezuela since a magnitude 7.6–7.7 event in 1900, in a region where the Caribbean and South American plates meet. The shaking hit a densely populated corridor including the capital, and the gap between the confirmed deaths and USGS modelling underscores the uncertainty over the eventual toll.

What comes next: Search-and-rescue operations continued on 25 June with people reported trapped under collapsed buildings, and the government's count is expected to rise as crews reach La Guaira and other areas. International teams from the US and other countries were mobilising, and authorities were assessing damage to the airport, transport and power networks.

items: Magnitudes: M7.2 foreshock and M7.5 mainshock, 39 seconds apart (USGS).
At least 164 dead and 971 injured per acting President Delcy Rodríguez, as of early 25 June.
Epicentre about 100 miles (160 km) west of Caracas, near Yumare, Yaracuy state.
USGS PAGER: red alert; modelled losses likely $10bn–$100bn.
Simón Bolívar International Airport damaged; Caracas Metro and rail suspended; nationwide school suspension.
Strongest Venezuelan quake since a ~M7.6 event in 1900.

items.title: Live updates: At least 164 dead after twin quakes in Venezuela, acting president says
Venezuela rocked by 7.5, 7.2 earthquakes: What happened and what we know
Death toll climbs to 164 killed, 971 injured in major earthquakes in Venezuela
M 7.5 - near Yumare, Venezuela (PAGER)

items.text: M7.2 then M7.5 39 seconds apart near Yumare west of Caracas — confirmed by the USGS event page and corroborated by Al Jazeera, NPR, NBC and CNN.
At least 164 dead and 971 injured attributed to acting President Delcy Rodríguez — reported consistently by CNN, NPR, Al Jazeera and NBC on 24–25 June.
Eventual death toll: USGS PAGER models thousands to tens of thousands, but confirmed deaths remained 164 as of 25 June; higher figures are modelling, not confirmed counts.

items: 1 · Magnitude/sequence consistency. Foreshock M7.2 below mainshock M7.5, 39 seconds apart — internally consistent and matches the USGS revision path. Check passes.
2 · Casualty sourcing. The 164/971 figures trace to a single named authority and explicitly exclude La Guaira; widely reported but single-origin — flagged Developing.
3 · Historical claim. "Strongest in over a century" rests on the 1900 M~7.6 comparison cited via USGS; the exact prior magnitude (7.6–7.7) is approximate.

stories.headline: US and Iran set 60-day roadmap; dispute inspections as Israel–Lebanon talks run in Washington
stories.summary: US and Iranian negotiators concluded Switzerland talks on a 60-day roadmap toward a final deal, with four working groups and a Strait of Hormuz communication line, while the two sides publicly disagreed over the timing of nuclear inspections.

confidence.label: Developing
confidence.why: — the roadmap, working groups and Hormuz channel are corroborated across wires, but the inspections timing is openly disputed and the Lebanon-talks outcome was still emerging

image.alt: Astronaut photograph looking down on the Strait of Hormuz between Iran and the Arabian Peninsula.
image.caption: The Strait of Hormuz, the waterway at the centre of the US–Iran communication channel. Photo: NASA · public domain

inline_image.alt: The Bürgenstock resort above Lake Lucerne, Switzerland.
inline_image.caption: The Bürgenstock resort in Switzerland, venue for the US–Iran technical talks. Photo: Asurnipal · CC BY-SA 4.0

article_blocks.html: The United States and Iran concluded a week of technical negotiations at the Bürgenstock resort in Switzerland, which Iran's delegation said wrapped up on 24 June 2026. The talks followed a high-level committee meeting on 21 June and built on a memorandum of understanding signed virtually by the US and Iranian presidents on 17 June.

According to a joint statement issued by mediators Pakistan and Qatar, the parties agreed on "a roadmap towards reaching a final deal within 60 days." Iran's Deputy Foreign Minister Kazem Gharibabadi, who led the Iranian delegation, said the sides reached agreement on the framework for future negotiations. Al Jazeera and the Express Tribune reported the creation of working groups covering sanctions termination, nuclear issues, reconstruction and economic development, reporting to a High-Level Committee.

On the Strait of Hormuz, negotiators established a communication line "to avoid incidents and miscommunication" with the stated aim of safe passage for commercial vessels during the 60-day period. Under the 17 June interim arrangement, the strait was set to run toll-free for at least 60 days, and the US lifted its naval blockade in mid-June.

Commercial traffic through the strait rose as the arrangement took effect. CNBC, citing Kpler data, reported tanker transits increasing after the deal's implementation; US Central Command said the waterway never fully closed, reporting 55 merchant ships transited on 20 June. Analysts note traffic remains below pre-war levels of more than 100 ships a day, and reports indicate the central deep-water channel still requires mine-clearing.

article_blocks.head: Timeline — the diplomatic track
article_blocks.sub: Tap an event to expand. Spacing reflects the time between events.

events.label: 17 Jun
events.title: Interim MoU signed
events.detail: US and Iranian presidents sign the memorandum virtually; the Strait of Hormuz set to reopen toll-free for at least 60 days.

events.label: 19 Jun
events.title: Lebanon ceasefire renewed
events.detail: Israel and Hezbollah renew a truce after a flare-up in southern Lebanon; a US official cites US, Qatar and Iran mediation.

events.label: 21 Jun
events.title: Talks open
events.detail: US, Iran and mediators Pakistan and Qatar open the Bürgenstock negotiations; a high-level committee meets.

events.label: 24 Jun
events.title: Roadmap agreed
events.detail: Iran says the technical talks concluded; working groups and the 60-day roadmap are set; the IAEA and Iran clash publicly on inspections.

events.label: 25 Jun
events.title: Lebanon track
events.detail: Final scheduled day of Israel–Lebanon talks in Washington on "pilot zones" in southern Lebanon.

article_blocks.html: The two governments publicly disagreed over nuclear inspections. IAEA Director General Rafael Mariano Grossi said on 24 June, speaking in Japan, that inspectors would visit Iranian enrichment sites under the interim deal. Gharibabadi countered the same day that such issues "will be reviewed and decided only within the framework of a final agreement and as a result of practical action by the other side to end all sanctions."

article_blocks.text: "Whether this happens the day after tomorrow or in one week or in 10 days, it's important, but not essential. This is going to happen."
article_blocks.src: — Rafael Mariano Grossi, IAEA Director General, 24 June 2026

article_blocks.html: On sanctions and assets, Iran said the US Treasury issued a 60-day waiver allowing oil and petrochemical sales, and Iranian officials announced agreement to release roughly $12 billion in frozen assets. NPR reported Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said personnel would be stationed in Qatar to oversee the unfrozen funds, which US officials said would be directed toward American agricultural products and medicines.

In parallel, the US convened an Israel–Lebanon track. After a flare-up in southern Lebanon, Israel and Hezbollah renewed a ceasefire on 19 June. A new round of Israel–Lebanon talks was held in Washington from 23 to 25 June, focused on "pilot zones" where the Lebanese Armed Forces would take control and Israeli troops would withdraw. The Times of Israel reported agreement to advance the pilot zones; a Hezbollah official told AFP the group rejected the ceasefire, and the Israeli military has not withdrawn from its buffer zone.

article_blocks.overview: Political-bias overview: coverage spans the spectrum; centre and wire outlets emphasise negotiation mechanics and energy-market effects, left-leaning outlets foreground sanctions relief and Lebanese casualties, and right-leaning outlets foreground Iranian concessions and inspection demands.
article_blocks.caption: Share of the gathered sources by lean - an approximate read, not a measure of the entire press.
article_blocks.ratings_html: Source leans: Reuters and AP rated Center, NPR Lean Left, Fox News Lean Right (AllSides). AllSides ratings ↗
lines: US outlets: CNBC and CNN stress the Hormuz communication line and tanker-traffic recovery; Fox News highlights US warnings to Iran and inspection demands.
Regional outlets: Al Jazeera and the Express Tribune foreground the mediators' roadmap and sanctions relief; the Times of Israel frames the Lebanon track around Hezbollah and pilot zones.

article_blocks.html: Why it matters: The Strait of Hormuz carries a large share of seaborne crude, so the communication channel and toll-free passage bear directly on global energy flows, while the inspections dispute tests whether the 60-day roadmap can produce a verified final deal. What comes next: working groups are to convene under the High-Level Committee, and the Israel–Lebanon talks were due to conclude in Washington on 25 June.

items: Parties agreed a roadmap "towards reaching a final deal within 60 days" (Pakistan–Qatar joint statement).
Four working groups: sanctions termination, nuclear, reconstruction and economic development.
A communication line set up for safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz; strait toll-free for at least 60 days.
US Treasury issued a 60-day waiver for Iranian oil sales; Iran cited release of about $12bn in frozen assets.
Israel–Lebanon talks ran in Washington 23–25 June on "pilot zones" in southern Lebanon.
Disputed: timing of IAEA inspections — under the interim deal (Grossi) vs only after a final deal (Iran).

items.title: US, Iran agree on 'roadmap' towards final deal in Switzerland talks
Dispute over nuclear inspections shows how U.S. and Iran are negotiating in public
Oil tanker traffic in Strait of Hormuz jumps after U.S. and Iran implement deal
US, Iran form four working groups in Switzerland talks

items.text: A 60-day roadmap toward a final deal was agreed at the Switzerland talks — corroborated by Al Jazeera, CNBC and NPR, citing the Pakistan–Qatar mediators' statement.
The two sides publicly disagree on when IAEA inspections occur — Grossi (24 June) says under the interim deal; Iran's Gharibabadi (24 June) says only after a final deal.

The ~$12bn figure for released assets and Qatar oversight rests largely on Iranian official statements relayed by NPR; independent US Treasury confirmation of the exact sum was not located in non-paywalled reporting.

agree: A 60-day roadmap and working groups under a High-Level Committee were established.
A Strait of Hormuz communication line was set up, and tanker traffic rose after the interim deal.
A US-brokered Israel–Lebanon track met in Washington 23–25 June on pilot zones.
contest: Whether IAEA inspections occur during the interim phase or only after a final agreement.
Whether the Strait of Hormuz effectively closed at any point (Iran's account vs CENTCOM transit figures).
Whether the Lebanon ceasefire is durable, given Hezbollah's rejection and the continued Israeli presence.

items: 1 · Accounts compared. Both sides confirm the roadmap, working groups and Hormuz channel, but characterise the inspection timeline differently; agreed and contested points are separated.

2 · "Safe passage" vs "reopening". Reporting distinguishes the toll-free reopening (17 June) from the 21 June communication line; CENTCOM says the strait never fully closed — "open" is contested in degree, not binary.
3 · Two 60-day clocks. The interim toll-free window dates from 17 June; the "final deal within 60 days" roadmap dates from the 21–24 June talks. The two are not identical and are not conflated.

stories.headline: France records hottest day on national scale since 1947 as Europe heat peaks
stories.summary: France's national temperature indicator reached 30.0°C on 24 June — its highest since the measure began in 1947 — and Météo-France placed 72 departments under red heatwave alert for 25 June as extreme heat extended across western Europe.

confidence.label: Developing
confidence.why: — core figures are reported by multiple outlets and primary agencies, but temperature records are provisional and the red-alert count rose through the week

image.alt: The Eiffel Tower in Paris against a clear sky.
image.caption: The Eiffel Tower in Paris, where visiting hours were restricted during the late-June 2026 heatwave. Photo: Benh Lieu Song · public domain

inline_image.alt: A mercury thermometer showing a high reading.
inline_image.caption: France's national temperature indicator reached a provisional 30.0°C on 24 June 2026. Photo: Anonimski · CC BY-SA 3.0

article_blocks.html: France recorded its hottest day on a national scale since the measure began in 1947 on Wednesday 24 June 2026, according to Météo-France. The national temperature indicator — an average of readings across roughly 30 stations over 24 hours — reached 30.0°C, surpassing the 29.8°C set the previous day. Both figures are provisional.

For Thursday 25 June, Météo-France placed 72 departments under red ("vigilance rouge") heatwave alert, up from 58 on Wednesday, with the remaining departments under orange or lower levels. The agency said the red alert covered more than 51 million residents.

Météo-France described the episode as an exceptional heatwave on a national scale, of a severity it said could approach that of August 2003, and said 25 June was expected to mark the peak. Afternoon highs of 40–42°C were forecast, with earlier-week readings including 43.6°C at Cazaux (Gironde) and 42.2°C at Nantes.

Authorities reported disruption across France. Thousands of homes experienced power cuts in northern France during the week, more than 2,000 schools closed or adjusted timetables, and operators restricted visiting hours at sites including the Eiffel Tower and the Louvre.

article_blocks.head: Timeline — the heat episode
article_blocks.sub: Tap an event to expand. Spacing reflects the time between events.

events.label: 18 Jun
events.title: Drowning deaths begin
events.detail: French officials later tallied about 40 drowning deaths between this date and 22 June.

events.label: 23 Jun
events.title: First record
events.detail: Météo-France logged a provisional national indicator of 29.8°C, later surpassed; 54 departments under red alert.

events.label: 24 Jun
events.title: Hottest day since 1947
events.detail: The national indicator reached 30.0°C; the UK recorded a provisional 36.1°C at Gosport.

events.label: 25 Jun
events.title: Alert peak
events.detail: 72 departments under red alert; Météo-France said Thursday should mark the heatwave's peak, with highs of 40–42°C.

events.label: 26 Jun
events.title: Forecast easing
events.detail: Atlantic air expected to bring gradual relief and thunderstorms from Friday.

article_blocks.html: French officials reported about 40 drowning deaths between 18 and 22 June, attributed in part to people swimming in unsupervised areas. The figure was given at an interministerial crisis meeting on 23 June; officials said many of those who died were young people.

article_blocks.text: This Thursday 25 June remains a heatwave day, and should mark the peak of this historic heatwave.

article_blocks.html: The heat extended across western and central Europe. Red or equivalent extreme-heat alerts were in place in Britain, Germany, Austria and Switzerland, with high temperatures also reported in Italy and Spain. Meteorologists attributed the episode to a heat dome — a persistent high-pressure system over the region.

In the United Kingdom, the Met Office said a provisional UK June maximum temperature record of 36.1°C was recorded at Gosport, Hampshire, on 24 June, exceeding the previous June record of 35.6°C set in 1957 and 1976. The Met Office said the figure remains subject to validation.

Why it matters: A national-average temperature record indicates heat that was widespread rather than confined to single locations, with implications for health systems, power grids, transport and outdoor labour across several countries at once. What comes next: Météo-France said cooler Atlantic air would arrive from the west from Thursday evening, bringing gradual relief and thunderstorms from Friday 26 June, while provisional records in France and the UK undergo validation.

items: National temperature indicator reached 30.0°C on 24 June, beating 29.8°C the day before (Météo-France, provisional; series began 1947).
72 departments under red heatwave alert on 25 June, up from 58; over 51 million residents covered.
Forecast highs of 40–42°C; peak readings included 43.6°C at Cazaux and 42.2°C at Nantes.
About 40 drowning deaths in France between 18 and 22 June.
UK provisional June record of 36.1°C at Gosport on 24 June, beating 35.6°C (1957/1976).
Red or equivalent extreme-heat alerts also in Britain, Germany, Austria and Switzerland.

items.title: Power outages in France as Europe bakes in record heat
Canicule : une baisse des températures par l'ouest pour le week-end
UK June maximum temperature record provisionally broken
France records its hottest day ever as Europe withers in heat wave

items.text: France's 30.0°C national indicator for 24 June is provisional, surpassing 29.8°C on 23 June (series from 1947) — confirmed across Météo-France, NPR and Al Jazeera.
The UK June record of 36.1°C at Gosport (24 June) is provisional and subject to validation, exceeding 35.6°C (1957/1976), per the Met Office.
The "~40 drowning deaths" total (18–22 June) is an official estimate; how many are specifically heat-related is not fully specified.

items: 1 · Average vs single station. The 30.0°C figure is a national 24-hour average across ~30 stations, distinct from local peaks such as 43.6°C at Cazaux; the two are kept separate.
2 · Provisional records labelled. Both the French national indicator and the UK 36.1°C figure are flagged as provisional/awaiting validation.
3 · Moving alert count. The red-alert total rose through the week (54 → 58 → 72); the 72 figure is used for 25 June with the trajectory noted.

stories.headline: Colombia's electoral council certifies de la Espriella as president-elect after challenge rejected
stories.summary: Colombia's electoral council on 24 June officially declared conservative Abelardo de la Espriella the winner of the 21 June runoff over Iván Cepeda, who conceded after the council rejected his party's challenge to roughly 33,000 polling stations.

confidence.label: Verified
confidence.why: — the 24 June certification and concession are reported consistently by France 24, Al Jazeera, PBS/AP and NBC News

image.alt: The Casa de Nariño, Colombia's presidential palace, in Bogotá.
image.caption: Colombia's president-elect is scheduled to be inaugurated on 7 August at the Casa de Nariño in Bogotá. Photo: Wikimedia Commons · CC BY-SA

inline_image.alt: Locator map showing Colombia highlighted on a globe centred on the Americas.
inline_image.caption: Colombia, where the electoral council certified the 21 June runoff result on 24 June. Photo: TUBS / Wikimedia Commons · CC BY-SA 3.0

article_blocks.html: Colombia's National Electoral Council (CNE) on 24 June 2026 officially declared Abelardo de la Espriella the president-elect for the 2026–2030 term, formalising the result of the 21 June runoff after a plenary session in Bogotá. The resolution also declared José Manuel Restrepo Abondano vice-president-elect.

The certification followed the concession of Iván Cepeda, the candidate of the Petro-aligned left, earlier on 24 June. Cepeda said he had decided to accept the result, framing the move as "an act of democratic responsibility, to contribute to harmony, peace and dialogue."

According to the CNE's official tally, de la Espriella won 12,960,166 votes (49.66%) against Cepeda's 12,708,312 (48.70%), a margin of about 251,854 votes — under one percentage point, and described by outlets as the narrowest in Colombia's recent presidential history.

The CNE rejected the opposition's petitions alleging discrepancies across roughly 33,000 polling stations, ruling the challenges lacked the requirements of the Electoral Code and that discrepancies fell within the normal statistical margin. The National Registry reported a 99.997% match between the election-night preliminary count and the official judge-led count.

article_blocks.head: Timeline — from first round to certification
article_blocks.sub: Tap an event to expand. Spacing reflects the time between events.

events.label: 31 May
events.title: First round
events.detail: De la Espriella and Cepeda advance to a runoff after the 31 May first round.

events.label: 21 Jun
events.title: Runoff held
events.detail: The preliminary count gives de la Espriella about 49.66%; Cepeda flags about 33,000 stations.

events.label: 23 Jun
events.title: Dispute
events.detail: President Petro alleges manipulation; the Registry reports 99.997% preliminary accuracy.

events.label: 24 Jun
events.title: Concession and certification
events.detail: Cepeda concedes; the CNE rejects the station challenges and certifies de la Espriella.

events.label: 7 Aug
events.title: Inauguration (scheduled)
events.detail: De la Espriella is due to take office for a four-year term.

article_blocks.text: "I have decided to accept the result of this process, which indicates that Abelardo de la Espriella is the new president."

article_blocks.html: President Gustavo Petro, who days earlier had alleged "electoral manipulation" and said US President Donald Trump's endorsement of de la Espriella nullified the vote, shifted on 24 June to acknowledging a "transition process" and his coming withdrawal from power — remarks widely read as accepting the outcome, though he stopped short of explicitly congratulating the winner.

De la Espriella, a 47-year-old lawyer and businessman who had never previously held office, was endorsed during the campaign by Trump and is described by international outlets as far-right or hard-right. His stated priorities, as reported, include a military offensive against armed groups, ending peace talks with rebel and criminal organisations, restoring diplomatic ties with Israel, boosting oil and gas, cutting taxes and shrinking the state.

article_blocks.overview: Political-bias overview: coverage converges on the verifiable facts — certification, concession and the margin — but differs in emphasis: right-leaning outlets foreground a Trump-backed win and a regional setback for the left, while left-leaning outlets foreground the "far-right" label, the narrowness of the result and the now-rejected fraud concerns.
article_blocks.caption: Share of the gathered sources by lean - an approximate read, not a measure of the entire press.
article_blocks.ratings_html: Source leans: Al Jazeera and NBC News rated Lean Left; France 24 and PBS/AP rated Center (AllSides). AllSides ratings ↗
lines: Left: stresses the "far-right" characterisation, Trump's endorsement and the contested count before concession.
Right: frames it as a Trump-endorsed conservative win and a fresh defeat for the Latin American left.

article_blocks.html: Why it matters: The certification ends days of uncertainty in a contest decided by roughly a quarter-million votes, completing a turn from Petro's left-wing government to a Trump-endorsed leader who has signalled shifts in security, foreign and economic policy. What comes next: de la Espriella is scheduled to be inaugurated on 7 August 2026, with transition teams expected to begin exchanging information on public finances and government programmes.

items: De la Espriella: 12,960,166 votes (49.66%), per the CNE official count.
Cepeda: 12,708,312 votes (48.70%).
Margin: about 251,854 votes — under one percentage point.
Challenge to roughly 33,000 polling stations rejected by the CNE on 24 June.
Preliminary-to-official count match reported at 99.997%.
Inauguration scheduled for 7 August 2026; VP-elect José Manuel Restrepo Abondano.

items.title: Colombia's left-wing candidate Cepeda concedes to De la Espriella
Ivan Cepeda concedes defeat in Colombia election, sealing right-wing win
Progressive candidate concedes Colombian presidential election to Trump-endorsed outsider
Cepeda concedes Colombian presidential election to Trump-backed outsider

items.text: The preliminary count is superseded by a certified result — France 24, Al Jazeera, PBS/AP and the Washington Post report the CNE declared de la Espriella president-elect on 24 June.
Cepeda's challenge to roughly 33,000 polling stations was rejected and he then conceded — reported by multiple outlets and the CNE resolution.
Petro's 23 June claim of proven "electoral manipulation" is an allegation attributed to him, contradicted by the Registry's reported 99.997% match and the CNE certification.

agree: De la Espriella won the runoff by roughly 251,000 votes; the CNE certified him president-elect on 24 June.
Cepeda conceded; inauguration is scheduled for 7 August 2026.
contest: Whether Trump's endorsement constituted improper foreign interference.
Whether the count was clean (Petro's manipulation allegation vs the CNE/Registry findings).

items: 1 · Status change. The 21 June figure was a preliminary count with no legal weight; the 24 June CNE figure is the certified result — the story leads with the certified state.
2 · Margin arithmetic. A ~251,854-vote lead against challenges to ~33,000 stations was found insufficient to overturn; the challenge was rejected rather than adjudicated vote-by-vote.
3 · "Winner" language. De la Espriella is described as president-elect only after CNE certification; pre-certification references are caveated as preliminary.

stories.headline: Asian memory-chip shares rise after Micron's record quarter; SK Hynix plans Nasdaq listing
stories.summary: AI-memory chip stocks across Asia rose in the 25 June session after Micron reported record fiscal third-quarter revenue of $41.46bn and SK Hynix disclosed a plan to raise up to about $29.4bn in a US listing.

confidence.label: Verified
confidence.why: — Micron's figures match the company's own release; SK Hynix's plan and the 25 June moves are corroborated across CNBC, Reuters and Korean financial press

image.alt: A row of computer RAM memory modules of various form factors on a white background.
image.caption: Computer memory modules. AI servers rely on DRAM and high-bandwidth memory supplied by Micron, Samsung and SK Hynix. Photo: Topory · CC BY-SA 3.0

inline_image.alt: A circular polished silicon wafer reflecting light.
inline_image.caption: A silicon wafer, the substrate from which memory chips are fabricated. Photo: Inductiveload · public domain

article_blocks.html: Memory-chip and chip-equipment shares across Asia rose in the 25 June 2026 trading session after Micron Technology reported record results for its fiscal third quarter and SK Hynix disclosed a plan to raise as much as about $29.4bn through a US share listing. South Korea's Kospi and Japan's Nikkei 225 both advanced during the session.

Micron, based in Boise, Idaho, said on 24 June (after the US close) that revenue for the quarter ended 28 May was $41.46bn, compared with $23.86bn the prior quarter and $9.30bn a year earlier. GAAP net income was $28.24bn, or $24.67 per diluted share; non-GAAP earnings were $25.11 per share. GAAP gross margin was 84.6% and non-GAAP gross margin 84.9%.

For its fiscal fourth quarter, Micron guided to revenue of about $50.0bn, plus or minus $1.0bn, with gross margin of approximately 86%. In prepared remarks, Micron said it had signed 16 Strategic Customer Agreements representing approximately $100bn in minimum contracted revenue, and expects to receive cash deposits and related commitments of about $22bn under the agreements signed so far. It described the contracts as multi-year, take-or-pay and non-cancellable.

SK Hynix, the South Korean memory maker, rose during the 25 June session; CNBC and a Reuters wire reported a gain of more than 12% at points in the session. The company said on 24 June it plans to issue up to 17.79 million new common shares as American depositary receipts on Nasdaq, in an offering provisionally valued at about 45.45 trillion won (roughly $29.4bn), with trading expected to begin around 10 July; it cautioned that the timetable and final size remain subject to bookbuilding.

article_blocks.head: Timeline — the chip print and its read-through
article_blocks.sub: Tap an event to expand. Spacing reflects the time between events.

events.label: 28 May
events.title: Quarter ends
events.detail: Micron's fiscal Q3 2026, for which it later reported $41.46bn revenue, closes.

events.label: 24 Jun
events.title: Micron reports
events.detail: Micron announces record Q3 results and ~$100bn in contracted revenue after the US close; shares rise about 13% in after-hours trading.

events.label: 24 Jun
events.title: SK Hynix discloses plan
events.detail: SK Hynix says it plans to raise up to ~$29.4bn via new shares as Nasdaq ADRs, listing targeted around 10 July.

events.label: 25 Jun
events.title: Asian chip shares rise
events.detail: SK Hynix, Samsung and Japanese chip-equipment makers rise; the Kospi and Nikkei advance.

events.label: 10 Jul
events.title: Targeted Nasdaq debut
events.detail: Tentative first trading date for the SK Hynix ADRs, subject to change.

article_blocks.text: "Micron's record fiscal Q3 financial results and even stronger outlook for Q4 reflect the strategic value of memory in the AI era."
article_blocks.src: — Sanjay Mehrotra, Chairman, President and CEO, Micron Technology, 24 June 2026

article_blocks.html: SK Hynix said it would direct the proceeds to facility investment, including the first fab at its Yongin cluster, an advanced-packaging plant in Cheongju and chipmaking equipment such as EUV lithography tools. BofA Securities, Citigroup, Goldman Sachs and J.P. Morgan are managing the offering.

Other chip-related shares rose in the session. Samsung Electronics rose more than 5%, and Taiwan's TSMC added under 1%. In Japan, Tokyo Electron rose about 7%, Advantest more than 4%, Lasertec more than 3% and SoftBank Group around 5%. Reported magnitudes vary because sources captured different intraday points.

Why it matters: Micron, Samsung and SK Hynix are the three large suppliers of DRAM and high-bandwidth memory used in AI servers. Micron's results, and its statement that AI-memory supply would stay tight through at least 2027, were read by investors as a signal of continued demand. What comes next: SK Hynix's ADR pricing and final size depend on bookbuilding, with listing targeted around 10 July; Micron next reports against guidance of about $50.0bn in revenue.

items: Micron fiscal Q3 revenue: $41.46bn, vs $9.30bn a year earlier (quarter ended 28 May).
Non-GAAP EPS $25.11; non-GAAP gross margin 84.9%.
16 Strategic Customer Agreements worth ~$100bn in minimum contracted revenue (multi-year, take-or-pay).
Micron Q4 guidance: revenue ~$50.0bn ±$1.0bn; gross margin ~86%.
SK Hynix plans to raise up to ~$29.4bn via Nasdaq ADRs; listing targeted ~10 July (tentative).
25 June moves: SK Hynix +>12%, Samsung +>5%, Tokyo Electron ~+7% (intraday).

items.title: Micron reports record results for the third quarter of fiscal 2026
SK Hynix surges after Micron earnings; Nasdaq listing planned
SK Hynix, Samsung rally after Micron results revive AI memory optimism
SK hynix to list ADRs on Nasdaq July 10, issuing 45 trillion won in new shares

items.text: Micron's revenue ($41,456m), non-GAAP EPS ($25.11) and gross margin (84.9%) are confirmed verbatim from Micron's own SEC-filed press release.
SK Hynix's listing plan (up to ~$29.4bn, ADRs, ~10 July target) is corroborated by CNBC and Seoul Economic Daily, citing the company's 24 June disclosure, with the timetable flagged as tentative.
The exact percentage of each Asian share move on 25 June varies by source (e.g. SK Hynix +>12% vs an intraday ~9.2%), reflecting different capture times rather than a confirmed close.

items: 1 · Revenue arithmetic. $41.46bn vs $9.30bn is about a 4.46x increase — consistent with the "nearly four times" descriptions in coverage.
2 · Contracts vs deposits. The ~$100bn is minimum contracted revenue across 16 multi-year agreements, not an annual figure; the ~$22bn refers to cash deposits/commitments — the two are kept separate.
3 · Session labels. Asian share moves are 25 June session figures; Micron's own ~13% move was in after-hours US trading on 24 June, not the 25 June Asian session.

stories.headline: CDC ends response to cruise-ship hantavirus outbreak after US monitoring clears
stories.summary: The US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention said on 24 June it had concluded its response to an Andes virus outbreak tied to the cruise ship M/V Hondius, after all US-exposed travellers completed a 42-day monitoring period without illness.

confidence.label: Verified
confidence.why: — the 24 June conclusion is reported via a CDC briefing and corroborated by Reuters, Al Jazeera and AJMC, though the total case count is given slightly differently across sources

image.alt: A long-tailed pygmy rice rat, the rodent reservoir of Andes virus, on the ground.
image.caption: The long-tailed pygmy rice rat, the natural reservoir of Andes virus in southern South America. Photo: Yamil Hussein E. · CC BY-SA 3.0

inline_image.alt: Labelled diagram of the structure of an Andes virus particle.
inline_image.caption: A schematic of the Andes virus particle, a hantavirus. Photo: ViralZone / SIB Swiss Institute of Bioinformatics · CC BY 4.0
inline_image.credit: ViralZone / SIB Swiss Institute of Bioinformatics

article_blocks.html: The US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) said on 24 June 2026 that it had concluded its public-health response to an outbreak of Andes virus, a type of hantavirus, associated with the expedition cruise ship M/V Hondius. The announcement was made at a CDC media briefing.

According to the CDC, all US citizens identified as having potential exposure aboard the vessel completed a 42-day monitoring period, which ended on 22 June. The agency said none of those monitored developed hantavirus disease and that no cases occurred in the United States as a result of the outbreak.

The CDC said 18 potentially exposed US travellers were repatriated in May and initially observed at the National Quarantine Unit at the University of Nebraska Medical Center, with some later completing monitoring at home. The agency said all remained symptom-free through the end of the observation period.

The outbreak was linked to a voyage that, according to the CDC and New England Journal of Medicine correspondence, ran from Ushuaia, Argentina, in early April through several South Atlantic islands before reaching Tenerife and later Rotterdam. The CDC and the European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control have described passengers and crew drawn from more than 20 countries.

article_blocks.head: Timeline — the outbreak and response
article_blocks.sub: Tap an event to expand. Spacing reflects the time between events.

events.label: 1 Apr
events.title: Cruise begins
events.detail: The M/V Hondius departs Ushuaia, Argentina, on a South Atlantic expedition voyage.

events.label: 2 May
events.title: Outbreak identified
events.detail: CDC situation reporting notes a hantavirus cluster linked to the vessel.

events.label: May
events.title: US travellers monitored
events.detail: 18 potentially exposed US travellers are brought to the National Quarantine Unit in Nebraska.

events.label: 22 Jun
events.title: Monitoring ends
events.detail: The last US-exposed travellers clear the 42-day observation period with no illness.

events.label: 24 Jun
events.title: Response concluded
events.detail: The CDC announces the formal conclusion of its hantavirus response.

article_blocks.text: "This week marks the successful conclusion of CDC's public health response to the hantavirus outbreak associated with the M/V Hondius cruise ship."
article_blocks.src: — CAPT Brendan Jackson, Acting Director, CDC Division of High Consequence Pathogens and Pathology, 24 June 2026

article_blocks.html: Andes virus is described by the CDC as the only hantavirus documented to spread person to person, through close, sustained contact. Hantavirus pulmonary syndrome, the severe respiratory illness it can cause, carries a case-fatality rate of roughly 38% among severe cases, according to the CDC, which cautioned that fatality estimates from this outbreak are uncertain because not everyone aboard was tested.

Three deaths have been reported among cases linked to the outbreak, all among non-US travellers, according to the CDC and Al Jazeera. The total number of laboratory-confirmed and probable cases has been reported as around 13 in CDC and NEJM materials, while some news outlets describe "more than a dozen"; these figures are reported here as attributed estimates.

CDC officials said disease ecologists were sent to Argentina to investigate the source and to trap rodents along areas connected to the itinerary, and that rodent samples tested to date were negative for Andes virus. The agency said the source of exposure remains under investigation.

Why it matters: Andes virus is the only hantavirus shown to transmit between people, so a cluster aboard a confined vessel prompted an international monitoring effort; the CDC's statement that no onward US transmission occurred marks the formal close of that effort. What comes next: the CDC said the origin investigation in South America continues, including further rodent and environmental sampling, with any additional findings expected through CDC and NEJM/ECDC channels.

items: 18 potentially exposed US travellers were monitored; none developed hantavirus disease (CDC, 24 June).
The monitoring period lasted 42 days and ended 22 June (CDC).
3 deaths reported among linked cases, all among non-US travellers (CDC; Al Jazeera).
Around 13 total cases (12 confirmed, 1 probable) cited in CDC/NEJM materials.
Hantavirus pulmonary syndrome case-fatality among severe cases is approximately 38% (CDC).
Andes virus is the only hantavirus documented to spread person to person (CDC).

items.title: Transcript – Update on CDC's Hantavirus Response – 6/24/26
Andes Virus Outbreak on a Cruise Ship: Current Situation
US public health agency concludes hantavirus response as outbreak eases
CDC officially ends hantavirus response as outbreak risk recedes

items.text: The CDC concluded its M/V Hondius hantavirus response on 24 June after a 42-day US monitoring period ending 22 June with no US cases — stated in the CDC briefing and corroborated by Al Jazeera, AJMC and Reuters.
Andes virus is the only hantavirus documented to spread person-to-person, and severe-case fatality is ~38% — per the CDC situation summary.
The precise total case count differs by source — CDC/NEJM cite ~13 (12 confirmed, 1 probable), some outlets say "more than a dozen" — reported here as an attributed estimate.

items: 1 · Association, not attribution. The three deaths are among non-US travellers; the story states only that no US cases occurred, without implying US monitoring prevented deaths.
2 · Date discipline. The news hook is the 24 June conclusion (monitoring ended 22 June), framed as "response concluded," not the April–May outbreak itself.
3 · Attribution. Case-fatality, person-to-person spread and case totals are attributed to the CDC/NEJM rather than stated as the briefing's own conclusions.

stories.headline: Scotland lose 0-3 to Brazil and finish third in Group C; qualification out of their hands
stories.summary: Brazil beat Scotland 3-0 through two Vinícius Júnior goals and one from Matheus Cunha, leaving Scotland third in Group C on three points and dependent on other groups to advance as a best third-placed side.

confidence.label: Verified
confidence.why: — the 0-3 score, scorers, standings and best-third scenario are reported consistently by ESPN, FOX, Yahoo and Wikipedia

image.alt: Scotland national football team players in dark blue kit during an international match.
image.caption: Scotland finished third in Group C at the 2026 World Cup. Photo: Daniel (Glasgow) · CC BY 2.0

inline_image.alt: Interior view of Hard Rock Stadium in Miami Gardens, Florida.
inline_image.caption: Hard Rock Stadium, Miami Gardens, venue for Scotland 0-3 Brazil. Photo: TheSoccerBoy · CC BY-SA 4.0

article_blocks.html: Brazil beat Scotland 3-0 in their final Group C match of the 2026 FIFA World Cup at Hard Rock Stadium in Miami Gardens, Florida, on 24 June 2026. The result confirmed Scotland's third-place finish in the group.
Vinícius Júnior scored in the 7th minute and added a second in first-half stoppage time. Matheus Cunha scored Brazil's third in the 60th minute. Neymar entered as a substitute in the 76th minute.

The win left Brazil top of Group C with seven points and a goal difference of plus-six. Morocco also finished on seven points, with a goal difference of plus-three after beating Haiti 4-2 the same day; Brazil placed first and Morocco second on goal difference.

Scotland finished third with three points and a goal difference of minus-three, having beaten Haiti 1-0 (John McGinn, 28') on 13 June, lost 1-0 to Morocco (Ismael Saibari, 2') on 19 June, and lost 3-0 to Brazil. Haiti finished fourth with no points.

article_blocks.head: Timeline — Scotland's group stage
article_blocks.sub: Tap an event to expand. Spacing reflects the time between events.

events.label: 13 Jun
events.title: Scotland 1-0 Haiti
events.detail: John McGinn scored in the 28th minute for Scotland's only group win.

events.label: 19 Jun
events.title: Scotland 0-1 Morocco
events.detail: Ismael Saibari scored in the 2nd minute.

events.label: 24 Jun
events.title: Scotland 0-3 Brazil
events.detail: Vinícius Júnior (7', 45'+3') and Matheus Cunha (60'); Scotland confirmed third.

events.label: 24 Jun
events.title: Group C complete
events.detail: Brazil first (7 pts, +6), Morocco second (7 pts, +3), Scotland third (3 pts, -3), Haiti fourth.

events.label: 27 Jun
events.title: Best-third places decided
events.detail: Third-placed standings across all groups finalise as remaining fixtures conclude.

article_blocks.text: "At this moment in time, it feels like we're going home. I think minus three is too much."

article_blocks.html: Under the 48-team format, the four group winners, four runners-up and the eight best third-placed teams across the 12 groups advance to the round of 32. Scotland, as a third-placed team, can still qualify but cannot influence their own position further, as their group fixtures are complete.

Scotland's qualification now depends on the third-placed records in the remaining groups; their three points and minus-three goal difference leave them open to being displaced by other third-placed sides whose group games conclude later in the week.
In the tournament's scoring race, Lionel Messi led with five goals from two matches, with Erling Haaland, Kylian Mbappé and Vinícius Júnior on four goals each.

Why it matters: Finishing third leaves Scotland's progression out of their own hands, with the margin of the Brazil defeat widening a goal-difference deficit that is the relevant tie-breaker for best third-placed places. What comes next: Scotland await the conclusion of the other groups' third-place standings; remaining group fixtures, including England's match against Panama on 27 June, continue through the week.

items: Final score: Scotland 0-3 Brazil, Hard Rock Stadium, 24 June.
Scorers: Vinícius Júnior 7' and 45'+3'; Matheus Cunha 60'. Neymar on at 76'.
Group C: Brazil 7 pts (+6), Morocco 7 pts (+3), Scotland 3 pts (-3), Haiti 0.
Scotland can advance only as one of the eight best third-placed teams; their fixtures are complete.
Scotland's path: beat Haiti 1-0, lost 1-0 to Morocco, lost 3-0 to Brazil.
Scoring race: Messi 5 goals; Haaland, Mbappé and Vinícius Júnior 4 each.

items.title: Scotland 0-3 Brazil (Jun 24, 2026) final score
2026 FIFA World Cup Group C
Steve Clarke on Scotland's World Cup chances after Brazil defeat
World Cup 2026 Golden Boot race: Messi, Mbappé, Haaland and Vinícius Jr

items.text: The 0-3 scoreline and scorers (Vinícius Júnior 7' and 45'+3', Matheus Cunha 60') are confirmed by ESPN's match page and Wikipedia, with FOX and Yahoo corroborating.
Group C final standings and Scotland's best-third scenario are confirmed by Wikipedia and ESPN; Scotland are not mathematically eliminated but cannot improve their own position.
Exact pairings/scorelines for some other final-round matches the same day are single-attributed across outlets and were not all independently confirmed.

items: 1 · Match date. The preponderance of sources (ESPN, Wikipedia, FOX, Yahoo) place the match on Wednesday 24 June; the dateline reflects 24 June, reported in this 25 June edition.
2 · England did not play. England's next match is reported as Saturday 27 June vs Panama; no claim is made that England played on 24–25 June.

3 · Elimination logic. A 0-3 loss does not mathematically eliminate Scotland but worsens their goal difference to -3, the operative best-third tie-breaker; scorers and minutes are internally consistent.

overview.headline: AI memory chips lift Asia after Micron's record quarter; oil holds below $70
overview.summary: Asian markets closed higher on 25 June on a memory-chip rally after Micron's record fiscal Q3, while oil stayed near four-month lows and the Fed's mid-June hold framed the rate backdrop.

confidence.label: Verified
confidence.why: — index and commodity levels match across two or more financial outlets; European levels are live intraday snapshots, labelled as such

image.alt: The trading floor of the New York Stock Exchange.
image.caption: Equities traded mixed into the chip rally. Photo: Kevin Hutchinson · CC BY 2.0

inline_image.alt: An offshore oil production platform at sea.
inline_image.caption: Oil eased below $70 a barrel through June. Photo: Agência Brasil (Petrobras) · CC BY 3.0 Brazil

article_blocks.html: Asian equity indices closed higher on Thursday 25 June, led by semiconductor stocks, after Micron Technology reported record results the previous evening. South Korea's Kospi closed up about 5.4%, and Japan's Nikkei 225 closed higher by roughly 3.8% to 4.6% depending on the outlet, with chip-equipment names leading.

The catalyst was Micron's fiscal third-quarter report, released after the US close on 24 June: record revenue of about $41.46bn, GAAP net income near $28.24bn, and fourth-quarter revenue guidance around $50bn. Micron shares rose about 13% in US after-hours trading on 24 June, after closing roughly flat in the regular session. The company cited a large volume of contracted, partly pre-paid AI-memory demand, reported at around $100bn.

The read-through lifted Asian memory makers in the 25 June session: SK Hynix closed up about 13%, helped also by a plan to raise as much as $29.4bn via a Nasdaq listing of American depositary receipts, and Samsung Electronics about 5%. Japanese suppliers Advantest and Tokyo Electron also closed higher.

The most recent completed US session was Wednesday 24 June; the 25 June US session had not opened at the time of writing (about 14:30 BST). At the 24 June close, the S&P 500 stood at 7,358.22 (down 0.10%), the Nasdaq Composite at 25,476.63 (down 0.43%) and the Dow Jones Industrial Average at 51,848.90 (up 0.35%) — a mixed session ahead of Micron's report.

European markets were mid-session on 25 June. The FTSE 100 traded around 10,540 (up roughly 0.7% intraday), having closed near 10,454 on 24 June; the DAX was lower intraday, quoted around 24,700–24,900 (down roughly 0.6% to 1.0%). These European figures are live intraday levels, not closing prints.

In commodities, oil has eased through June on Iran de-escalation and the reopening of tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz. As of 25 June, Brent crude was around $73 a barrel and WTI around $69 — below $70 and near their lowest since late February. Gold was lower, near $3,980–$4,005 a troy ounce after falling more than 3% on 24 June, and US natural gas was around $3.25 per million British thermal units.

On rates, the Federal Open Market Committee held the federal funds target at 3.50%–3.75% on 17 June, a fourth consecutive hold. The updated projections were more hawkish than March, with the median path implying rates ending the year slightly higher and several participants anticipating at least one increase, against a backdrop of elevated inflation partly tied to energy.

Net, the week's tone was set by a single chip print rippling outward — record Micron numbers reviving the AI-memory trade across Asia — set against still-soft oil, a pullback in gold, and a Fed on hold but signalling less easing ahead.

items: S&P 500: 7,358.22, down 0.10% — 24 June close (most recent completed US session).
Nasdaq Composite: 25,476.63, down 0.43% — 24 June close.
Kospi: up about 5.4% — 25 June close (Seoul).
FTSE 100: around 10,540, up about 0.7% — 25 June intraday (live).
Brent crude ~$73/bbl; WTI ~$69/bbl — 25 June (below $70).
Gold ~$3,980–$4,005/oz — 25 June, after a >3% drop on 24 June.

items.title: SK Hynix, Samsung rally after Micron results revive AI memory optimism
Stock Market Today, June 24: Micron surges after hours as Nasdaq stems losses
Fed interest rate decision June 2026: Fed holds rates steady

items: 1 · Session labels. US figures are tagged 24 June close (the 25 June US session had not opened); Asian figures are 25 June closes; European figures are 25 June intraday. No stale close is presented as live.

2 · Level/direction consistency. The S&P down 0.10% with the Dow up 0.35% is an internally consistent mixed close; the FTSE near 10,540 against a ~10,454 prior close is consistent with a ~0.7% intraday gain.

movers.dateline: US: Wed 24 Jun close · Asia: 25 Jun close · Europe: 25 Jun intraday (live)
movers.headline: Chip rally lifts Asia; US figures are Wednesday's close, Europe is live mid-session
movers.note_html: Each figure is shown for its own stated trading session — US values are the 24 June close (the 25 June US session had not opened at ~14:30 BST), Asian values are 25 June closes, and European values are live 25 June intraday levels still moving at publication. These are not uniformly live.

groups.note_html: Micron's +13% is after-hours on 24 June (it closed roughly flat in the regular session). Cerebras (CBRS) also fell on its first earnings as a public company; a precise figure was not sourced, so none is asserted.
UK single-stock movers are from the 24 June close; named FTSE 100 movers for the 25 June intraday session could not be independently sourced from two outlets.

archive.title: Twin earthquakes near Caracas kill at least 164, Venezuela's acting president says
archive.summary: An M7.2 foreshock and an M7.5 mainshock 39 seconds apart struck northern Venezuela on 24 June, killing at least 164 and injuring 971 by the government's early-Thursday count, with the toll expected to rise.

archive.title: US and Iran set 60-day roadmap; dispute inspections as Israel–Lebanon talks run in Washington
archive.summary: US and Iranian negotiators concluded Switzerland talks on a 60-day roadmap toward a final deal, with four working groups and a Strait of Hormuz communication line, while the two sides publicly disagreed over the timing of nuclear inspections.

archive.title: France records hottest day on national scale since 1947 as Europe heat peaks
archive.summary: France's national temperature indicator reached 30.0°C on 24 June — its highest since the measure began in 1947 — and Météo-France placed 72 departments under red heatwave alert for 25 June as extreme heat extended across western Europe.

archive.title: Colombia's electoral council certifies de la Espriella as president-elect after challenge rejected
archive.summary: Colombia's electoral council on 24 June officially declared conservative Abelardo de la Espriella the winner of the 21 June runoff over Iván Cepeda, who conceded after the council rejected his party's challenge to roughly 33,000 polling stations.

archive.summary: Asian markets closed higher on 25 June on a memory-chip rally after Micron's record fiscal Q3, while oil stayed near four-month lows and the Fed's mid-June hold framed the rate backdrop.
